title: Alnylam — ATTR — Knowledge Doc
html:
ATTR Amyloidosis: Disease & Competitive Landscape

1. Disease Overview

Transthyretin (TTR) amyloidosis (ATTR amyloidosis) is a progressive, multisystem disease caused by extracellular deposition of misfolded transthyretin monomers as insoluble amyloid fibrils in tissues including the heart, peripheral nerves, gastrointestinal tract, and kidneys. TTR is a transport protein produced primarily in the liver that carries thyroxine and retinol-binding protein.

Two Forms of ATTR Amyloidosis

Feature Hereditary ATTR (hATTR / ATTRv) Wild-Type ATTR (wtATTR)
Cause >130 known TTR gene mutations (e.g., V30M, V122I, T60A) Age-related misfolding of normal TTR protein
Age of onset 30-70 years depending on mutation Typically >65 years, predominantly male
Primary manifestations Polyneuropathy (hATTR-PN), cardiomyopathy (hATTR-CM), or mixed Almost exclusively cardiomyopathy (wtATTR-CM)
Prevalence ~50,000 globally; endemic in Portugal, Sweden, Japan, Brazil Estimated 200,000-300,000 in US alone; dramatically under-diagnosed
Inheritance Autosomal dominant Not inherited

Clinical Phenotypes

ATTR Cardiomyopathy (ATTR-CM): The larger market opportunity. Manifests as heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), conduction abnormalities, atrial fibrillation, and carpal tunnel syndrome. The THAOS registry shows ~41% of symptomatic ATTR patients present with predominantly cardiac phenotype. Among older HFpEF patients, 7-13% may have undiagnosed wtATTR-CM. Two-year mortality risk ranges from 10-30% (wtATTR-CM) to 10-50% (variant ATTR-CM). ATTR-CM is the primary commercial battleground, with an estimated US prevalence of ~200,000 patients.

ATTR Polyneuropathy (hATTR-PN / ATTRv-PN): Hereditary only. Progressive sensorimotor and autonomic neuropathy. Staged by FAP (Familial Amyloid Polyneuropathy) staging from Stage 1 (ambulatory) to Stage 3 (wheelchair-bound). Endemic populations exist in Portugal (V30M mutation), Japan (V30M), Sweden (V30M), and Brazil. Smaller market (~50,000 globally) but the original indication for RNAi therapies.

Mixed phenotype: ~17% of patients present with both polyneuropathy and cardiomyopathy. This population is strategically important because it bridges both indications and is where RNAi therapies (which address both) have a differentiation advantage over stabilizers (which are approved only for ATTR-CM).

Diagnosis Landscape

Diagnosis rates are improving but remain low relative to true prevalence. Key diagnostic modalities:

  • Cardiac imaging: Technetium pyrophosphate (99mTc-PYP) bone scintigraphy for non-invasive diagnosis of ATTR-CM (Grade 2/3 uptake is diagnostic in the absence of a monoclonal protein)
  • Genetic testing: TTR gene sequencing to distinguish hereditary from wild-type
  • Endomyocardial biopsy: Gold standard but invasive; used when non-invasive workup is equivocal
  • Biomarkers: NT-proBNP, troponin T/I for cardiac involvement staging

Alnylam's 2025 strategic imperative #2 focuses on accelerating diagnosis through education on the rapidly progressive nature of disease and multisystem burden.

2. Therapeutic Landscape

Mechanism Classes

The ATTR treatment landscape divides into two fundamental mechanism classes:

TTR Silencers (Gene Silencing): Reduce production of TTR protein at the source (liver). Two sub-types:

  • RNAi (RNA interference): AMVUTTRA (vutrisiran), ONPATTRO (patisiran) -- Alnylam
  • ASO (antisense oligonucleotide): Wainua/Wainzua (eplontersen) -- Ionis/AstraZeneca; TEGSEDI (inotersen) -- legacy

TTR Stabilizers: Bind to TTR tetramer to prevent dissociation into amyloidogenic monomers:

  • Tafamidis: VYNDAQEL/VYNDAMAX (Pfizer) -- dominant incumbent in ATTR-CM
  • Acoramidis: ATTRUBY (BridgeBio) / BEYONTTRA (ex-US) -- new entrant, claims superior stabilization

Product Profiles

AMVUTTRA (vutrisiran) -- Alnylam

  • Mechanism: Small interfering RNA (siRNA) targeting TTR mRNA via RNA interference; GalNAc-conjugated for liver-targeted delivery
  • Administration: Subcutaneous injection, once every 3 months (quarterly)
  • Approved indications:
    • hATTR-PN (polyneuropathy): Approved June 2022 (US), September 2022 (EU)
    • ATTR-CM (cardiomyopathy): Approved March 20, 2025 (US -- first RNAi approved for ATTR-CM), June 2025 (EU)
  • Key clinical data:
    • HELIOS-A (hATTR-PN): Non-inferior to ONPATTRO; 48% of patients improved neuropathy from baseline; mean 88% TTR knockdown
    • HELIOS-B (ATTR-CM): Transformational result. 28% reduction in all-cause mortality + recurrent CV events (overall population); 33% reduction in monotherapy arm. Statistically significant improvements in 6-minute walk test, KCCQ, and NT-proBNP
  • 2025 Revenue: $2,314 million (AMVUTTRA alone); TTR franchise total $2,487 million (including ONPATTRO $173M)
  • 2026 Guidance: TTR franchise $4,400-$4,700 million (83% growth YoY at midpoint)
  • Patients on therapy: >12,000 as of early 2026
  • Key differentiators: Only product approved for both ATTR-CM and hATTR-PN; quarterly SC dosing; 88% TTR reduction (highest among silencers); addresses both wild-type and variant TTR

ONPATTRO (patisiran) -- Alnylam

  • Mechanism: siRNA targeting TTR mRNA; lipid nanoparticle (LNP) formulation
  • Administration: IV infusion every 3 weeks; requires premedication (corticosteroid, acetaminophen, H1 and H2 blockers)
  • Approved indication: hATTR-PN (August 2018 US; first RNAi therapeutic ever approved)
  • Status: Being gradually transitioned to AMVUTTRA. Revenue declining: $173M in 2025 (vs. $2,314M for AMVUTTRA)
  • Key data: APOLLO trial -- significant improvement vs. placebo in mNIS+7 and Norfolk QoL-DN
  • Role in franchise: Legacy product, still relevant in France and certain markets where it was first-to-market and has strong neurologist loyalty. Alnylam reps in France equally prioritize AMVUTTRA and ONPATTRO

VYNDAQEL/VYNDAMAX (tafamidis) -- Pfizer

  • Mechanism: TTR stabilizer; binds to thyroxine-binding sites on TTR tetramer
  • Administration: Oral, once daily. VYNDAQEL 20mg (original) and VYNDAMAX 61mg (free acid, bioequivalent to 80mg meglumine)
  • Approved indications: ATTR-CM (May 2019 US); hATTR-PN in some markets
  • Key clinical data: ATTR-ACT trial: 30% reduction in all-cause mortality over 30 months; 32% reduction in CV-related hospitalizations
  • 2024 Revenue: $5.45 billion (franchise); dominant market leader in ATTR-CM
  • Market dynamics: VYNDAQEL being discontinued in US (end of 2025); VYNDAMAX continues. Facing competition from ATTRUBY and AMVUTTRA. Patent expiration approaching 2028
  • Competitive position: Incumbent advantage; oral convenience; 5-year long-term follow-up data. Weaknesses: slower onset of benefit (18+ months to separation from placebo), does not address polyneuropathy, generic threat approaching

ATTRUBY (acoramidis) -- BridgeBio / BEYONTTRA (ex-US)

  • Mechanism: TTR stabilizer; binds with higher affinity and selectivity than tafamidis
  • Administration: Oral, once daily (two 200mg capsules)
  • Approved: November 2024 (US) for ATTR-CM
  • Key clinical data: ATTRibute-CM trial: significant reduction in all-cause mortality; claims >90% TTR stabilization (vs. ~80% for tafamidis); benefit observed as early as 1 month
  • 2025 Revenue: $362.4 million (full year); rapid uptake
  • Patients: 7,804 unique prescriptions from 1,856 prescribers as of Feb 2026
  • Competitive position: Positioned as "best-in-class stabilizer" -- claims faster onset, greater stabilization. Rapidly gaining share in newly diagnosed ATTR-CM. Commercialized internationally by Bayer (Europe) and Alexion (Japan)

Wainua / Wainzua (eplontersen) -- Ionis/AstraZeneca

  • Mechanism: Ligand-conjugated antisense oligonucleotide (LICA); GalNAc-conjugated for liver targeting
  • Administration: SC injection, once monthly via auto-injector (self-administered)
  • Approved: December 2023 (US) for hATTR-PN; EU approval for hATTR-PN (2024)
  • ATTR-CM: CARDIO-TTRansform Phase 3 study fully enrolled with >1,400 patients (largest ATTR-CM trial ever). Primary completion date extended to April 2026; data expected mid-2026
  • FDA Fast Track designation: Granted for ATTR-CM
  • Competitive position: Only self-administered product (auto-injector); monthly dosing vs. quarterly for AMVUTTRA. Direct competitor to AMVUTTRA in polyneuropathy. ATTR-CM data pending -- this is the major upcoming catalyst. Weaker TTR knockdown (~80% at trough vs. ~88% sustained for AMVUTTRA)

TEGSEDI (inotersen) -- Ionis (legacy)

  • Mechanism: First-generation ASO targeting TTR mRNA
  • Administration: SC weekly, self-administered; requires platelet monitoring
  • Status: Being supplanted by Wainua (eplontersen). Still marketed in some European markets
  • Safety concerns: Risk of thrombocytopenia, glomerulonephritis; requires REMS program in US
  • Relevance: Declining. Still tracked in ZoomRx France PET data (Q4 2023) but disappearing from later waves

3. Mechanism of Action Deep Dive

Understanding the fundamental mechanism differences is critical for interpreting promotional messaging effectiveness and physician preference data.

Gene Silencing (RNAi and ASO)

RNA interference (RNAi) -- AMVUTTRA and ONPATTRO: Small interfering RNA (siRNA) molecules silence TTR gene expression in hepatocytes. The siRNA binds to complementary TTR messenger RNA (mRNA) and recruits the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC), which cleaves the mRNA and prevents TTR protein synthesis. The result is a dramatic reduction in circulating TTR protein -- both mutant and wild-type. AMVUTTRA uses GalNAc conjugation for efficient liver targeting via the asialoglycoprotein receptor (ASGPR), enabling subcutaneous delivery. ONPATTRO uses lipid nanoparticle (LNP) encapsulation, which requires IV infusion with premedication to prevent infusion-related reactions.

Key pharmacology: AMVUTTRA achieves ~88% mean TTR knockdown within 3 weeks, sustained with quarterly dosing. This rapid and deep suppression is the basis of Alnylam's "rapid knockdown" messaging narrative -- one of the most motivating messages in PET data across all markets, yet consistently under-delivered by reps.

Antisense oligonucleotides (ASO) -- Wainua and TEGSEDI: Single-stranded modified DNA or RNA molecules that bind complementary TTR mRNA via Watson-Crick base pairing and recruit RNase H1 to degrade the mRNA. Wainua (eplontersen) uses ligand-conjugated antisense (LICA) technology with GalNAc conjugation, similar to AMVUTTRA's liver targeting approach but through a different gene silencing pathway. Achieves ~80% TTR reduction at trough. TEGSEDI (inotersen) was the first-generation ASO without conjugation, requiring higher doses and carrying more safety risks (thrombocytopenia, glomerulonephritis).

TTR Stabilization (Tafamidis and Acoramidis)

Stabilizers bind to the thyroxine-binding sites on the TTR tetramer, preventing its dissociation into pro-amyloidogenic monomers. Unlike silencers, stabilizers do not reduce TTR production -- they keep existing TTR in its native, non-toxic tetrameric form. This means amyloid deposits already present continue to exist but further deposition is slowed.

Tafamidis (VYNDAQEL/VYNDAMAX): First-generation stabilizer. Achieves ~80% TTR stabilization. The ATTR-ACT trial showed a 30% mortality reduction over 30 months, but the Kaplan-Meier curves did not separate until ~18 months -- a slower onset than physicians prefer. Available as oral once-daily formulation, which is the primary convenience advantage.

Acoramidis (ATTRUBY/BEYONTTRA): Second-generation stabilizer. Claims >90% TTR stabilization (higher selectivity and binding affinity than tafamidis). The ATTRibute-CM trial showed clinical benefit as early as 1 month, addressing the tafamidis "slow onset" weakness. This "fastest onset" positioning is BridgeBio's primary competitive weapon against both tafamidis and AMVUTTRA.

Silencer vs. Stabilizer: The Strategic Choice

The fundamental clinical question is whether reducing TTR production (silencing) or stabilizing existing TTR (stabilization) produces better long-term outcomes. Arguments for each:

Silencing advantages:

  • Addresses the root cause by reducing TTR production at the source
  • Effective against both mutant and wild-type TTR equally
  • Covers both cardiac and neurological manifestations
  • 88% reduction in circulating TTR (vs. preventing ~80-90% from misfolding)
  • HELIOS-B showed mortality benefit in ATTR-CM, validating the cardiac efficacy of the gene silencing approach

Stabilizer advantages:

  • Oral administration (daily pill vs. injection)
  • No vitamin A supplementation required (silencers reduce retinol-binding protein alongside TTR)
  • Longer real-world track record (tafamidis approved 2019 for ATTR-CM)
  • Physician familiarity, especially among cardiologists accustomed to oral cardiovascular medications
  • Potentially lower cost (generic tafamidis expected post-2028)

The emerging consensus in treatment guidelines (ACC 2025 Concise Clinical Guidance) is that both classes have a role, with the treatment choice depending on disease phenotype (CM vs. PN vs. mixed), severity, patient preference for administration route, and increasingly, the potential for combination therapy.

4. Competitive Dynamics

The Three-Way Battle in ATTR-CM (2025-2026)

The ATTR-CM market is now a three-way competition, with Wainua potentially entering as a fourth:

  1. VYNDAMAX (tafamidis) -- the incumbent: $5.45B franchise (2024). Oral convenience. But losing share to newer entrants. Patent cliff approaching 2028. Pfizer discontinuing VYNDAQEL (low-dose) in US by end 2025, consolidating to VYNDAMAX only

  2. AMVUTTRA (vutrisiran) -- the disruptor: HELIOS-B data positions it as first disease-modifying therapy with mortality benefit via gene silencing. Quarterly SC dosing. Only product bridging CM and PN. Explosive revenue growth ($2.3B in 2025, guided to $4.4-4.7B in 2026)

  3. ATTRUBY (acoramidis) -- the challenger: "Better stabilizer" narrative. Oral daily. $362M in first full year. Gaining traction with newly diagnosed patients. Claims faster onset (1 month) and >90% stabilization

  4. Wainua (eplontersen) -- the pending entrant: CARDIO-TTRansform data expected mid-2026. Monthly SC self-administered. If positive, creates direct head-to-head silencer competition with AMVUTTRA

Key Strategic Questions

  • Silencer vs. Stabilizer: Will the market stratify by mechanism, with silencers preferred for mixed phenotype/PN and stabilizers preferred for pure CM? Or will HELIOS-B's mortality data position AMVUTTRA as universal first-line?
  • Combination therapy: Will silencer + stabilizer combinations become standard of care? ZoomRx PCA data already tracks AMVUTTRA + VYNDAMAX and AMVUTTRA + ATTRUBY combinations
  • Wainua's ATTR-CM entry: If CARDIO-TTRansform is positive, Wainua becomes the only self-administered silencer with ATTR-CM approval. Self-administration is a meaningful differentiator for patient convenience
  • Access and affordability: Insurance coverage, formulary status, and out-of-pocket costs are significant competitive battlegrounds. Alnylam reports 99% US coverage and 70% of patients paying $0 out-of-pocket

5. Market Sizing and Patient Journey

US Market Opportunity

The US ATTR market is stratified by indication and diagnosis status:

Segment Estimated Patients Diagnosed On Treatment Key Insight
wtATTR-CM ~200,000 ~40,000-50,000 ~25,000-30,000 Largest opportunity; massive under-diagnosis
hATTR-CM (V122I and other variants) ~20,000-30,000 ~5,000-10,000 ~3,000-5,000 V122I mutation prevalent in African Americans (~3.4% carrier frequency)
hATTR-PN ~10,000 (US) ~3,000-5,000 ~2,000-3,000 Smaller but fully penetrated by RNAi therapies
Mixed phenotype ~5,000-10,000 Variable Variable Strategically important for RNAi differentiation

The diagnosed population is growing 15-20% annually due to improved awareness, non-invasive cardiac imaging (Tc-PYP scintigraphy), and increased genetic testing. Alnylam estimates ~200,000 ATTR-CM patients in the US; at current diagnosis rates, the addressable treated population is a fraction of this. This diagnosis gap is why Alnylam's Strategic Imperative #2 focuses on "driving urgency to diagnose."

International Market Dynamics

Based on ZoomRx international PET data, the key ex-US markets each have distinct characteristics:

France: Strong neurology heritage. ONPATTRO was established early, creating deep Alnylam loyalty among neurologists. Alnylam's total SOV leads Pfizer (55% vs. 45% among neurologists in Q2 2024). Average hATTR patient load per physician is relatively high (~6-8 patients). Practice setting skewed community (37%) vs. academic (63%) in W1, with neuro-only recruitment in W2.

Italy: Balanced between cardiology and neurology. Higher academic setting (70%). AMVUTTRA's reach declined between waves (58% to 51%), while VYNDAQEL maintained reach (62%). Average patient load increased from 9 to 14 between waves, suggesting growing diagnosis rates. NPS for both products declined between waves, suggesting increased competitive noise.

Germany: Community-practice dominant (69% in Q4 2024). Smaller average patient loads (~3-4). Alnylam and Pfizer at near-parity in reach (71% each) but Pfizer maintains marginal SOV advantage (53% vs. 47%). Germany is price-sensitive -- Alnylam flagged ~$25M quarterly revenue reduction from German price cuts in 2026.

Spain: Alnylam's strongest international market. Predominantly academic setting (96%). AMVUTTRA achieved the most dramatic competitive turnaround -- SOV flipped from 35% to 56% between Q4 2023 and Q4 2024. Average patient load increased from 6 to 13, suggesting rapidly growing diagnosis. Alnylam reach hit 91%, the highest of any market.

Japan: Pfizer-dominant. Tafamidis has historical presence (approved for hATTR-PN before many markets). Very low average patient loads (~2 per physician). VYNDAQEL/VYNMAC maintains 59% SOV and 84% reach vs. AMVUTTRA's 41% SOV and 72% reach. However, AMVUTTRA reps drive stronger perception improvement when they do reach physicians (78% vs. 57%), indicating an access problem rather than a message problem.

Patient Journey in ATTR-CM

The typical ATTR-CM patient journey, as informed by ZoomRx PCA data and the ADOPT funnel framework:

  1. Suspicion and Referral: Patient presents with HFpEF, unexplained LVH, aortic stenosis, carpal tunnel syndrome, or spinal stenosis. Increasingly, advanced imaging and biomarkers trigger ATTR workup. Mean time from symptom onset to diagnosis: 2-4 years (improving but still delayed)

  2. Diagnosis: Tc-PYP scintigraphy (non-invasive, high sensitivity/specificity). If Grade 2/3 uptake + no monoclonal protein on serum/urine testing, ATTR-CM can be diagnosed without biopsy. Genetic testing to distinguish wtATTR from hATTR

  3. Treatment Decision: Historically tafamidis was the only disease-modifying option. Now three choices: VYNDAMAX (oral stabilizer), ATTRUBY (oral stabilizer, "better"), AMVUTTRA (SC injection, silencer). Treatment selection increasingly influenced by phenotype (pure CM vs. mixed), severity, age, and comorbidities

  4. Ongoing Management: Heart failure optimization (diuretics, MRAs, SGLT2i), arrhythmia management, multidisciplinary care (cardiology, neurology, genetic counseling). Monitoring TTR levels (for silencer-treated patients), NT-proBNP, 6-minute walk test, KCCQ

  5. Therapy Optimization: Emerging question of whether to add a second agent (silencer + stabilizer) or switch MOA class if progression occurs. ZoomRx PCA already capturing combination use patterns

6. Alnylam Strategic Imperatives (2025-2026)

Based on the Q1 2025 Integration of Market Research deck, Alnylam's US TTR Business Unit operates on four strategic imperatives:

  1. Establish AMVUTTRA as first-line treatment of choice -- Differentiate as new SOC in 1L ATTR-CM, foundational therapy across treatment paradigm, RNAi as MOA of choice, rapid TTR knockdown narrative, real-world experience
  2. Drive urgency to diagnose, treat, and optimize therapy -- Accelerate diagnosis via education on disease progression, support understanding of progression on therapy, optimize clinical workflows
  3. Optimize customer experience and minimize non-clinical barriers -- Formulary approval, reduce administrative/site barriers, build confidence in access/affordability
  4. Establish leadership in cardiology/ATTR -- Expand presence in cardiology and heart failure, build champions, build interest in TTR pipeline (ALN-TTRsc04 and beyond)

Strategic Imperative to Research Mapping

The Q1 2025 Integration deck maps each imperative to specific ZoomRx research deliverables:

Imperative 1 (AMVUTTRA as 1L): ATU tracks AMVUTTRA awareness and perceptions for ATTR-CM, patient share, and drivers/barriers. PET tracks branded and unbranded message recall, likelihood to prescribe post rep interaction, and product perceptions of MOA (stabilizer vs. silencer). PCA captures treatment journey, consideration set, source of share, and key drivers to prescribing.

Imperative 2 (Diagnosis urgency): ATU tracks HCP alignment to disease beliefs, tests and assessments used. PET tracks recall of disease state messages and impact of disease education on familiarity. PCA tracks treatment journey and usage of tests across patient profiles.

Imperative 3 (Minimize barriers): ATU tracks perceptions of administration burden, access/affordability. PET tracks recall of access messages and blinded message association for access/cost content.

Imperative 4 (Cardiology leadership): ATU tracks brand equity. PET tracks manufacturer perceptions and impact on prescribing behavior. This is the imperative most directly tied to the cardiologist reach gap identified in international PET data.

7. ZoomRx Research Program Architecture

ZoomRx runs three complementary tracking studies for the Alnylam ATTR franchise:

US Studies

  • PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracker): Quarterly personal promotions + semi-annual NPP. Tracks AMVUTTRA-CM, AMVUTTRA-PN, VYNDAMAX, ATTRUBY, and WAINUA. 90 interactions per product per quarter (CM), 60 per semi-annual wave (PN). Measures reach, frequency, SOV, message recall, message effectiveness, rep performance, product perception change, likelihood to Rx
  • ATU (Awareness, Trial, Usage): Quarterly for ATTR-CM (90 cardiologists), one wave for hATTR-PN (80 physicians, 50/50 cardio/neuro). Tracks awareness, familiarity, drivers/barriers, product performance, stated vs. derived importance, NPS
  • PCA (Patient Chart Audit): Semi-annual. 300 charts CM+Mixed, 100 charts PN. Treatment journey, consideration set, source of share, new-to-brand Rx, combination therapy patterns

International PET Studies

Multi-country promotional effectiveness tracking across France, Italy, Germany, Spain, and Japan. Two waves completed:

  • Wave 1 (Q4 2023): France, Italy, Japan, Germany, Spain -- baseline measurements
  • Wave 2 (Q2 2024 for France/Italy; Q4 2024 for Spain/Germany/Japan) -- trend tracking

Products tracked: AMVUTTRA, ONPATTRO (France only), VYNDAQEL/VYNDAMAX, TEGSEDI (France W1 only)

Integrated Analysis

Quarterly integration of PET+ATU+PCA data into the HCP Adoption (ADOPT) funnel framework: Awareness --> Depth of Knowledge --> Overall Product Perception --> Product Usage --> Treatment Advocacy. Each study feeds different stages of the funnel.

8. Key Metrics and Definitions

  • SOV (Share of Voice): Ratio of brand details to total market details. Primary competitive metric
  • Message Effectiveness (ME): Geometric mean of Differentiation, Believability, and Motivation scores (6,7 on 7-point scale)
  • Reach: % of physicians reporting at least one rep interaction in last 60 days
  • Frequency: Average number of interactions per physician in last 60 days
  • NPS (Net Promoter Score): % promoters (9-10) minus % detractors (0-6) on likelihood to recommend
  • TTR Knockdown: % reduction in serum TTR protein levels -- key efficacy biomarker for silencers. AMVUTTRA achieves ~88% mean knockdown
  • TTR Stabilization: % of TTR tetramer stabilized -- key efficacy biomarker for stabilizers. ATTRUBY claims >90%, tafamidis ~80%

9. Pipeline and Future Catalysts

Timeline Event Impact
Mid-2026 CARDIO-TTRansform (eplontersen/Wainua in ATTR-CM) data readout If positive, creates direct silencer-vs-silencer competition. If negative, consolidates AMVUTTRA's silencer monopoly in ATTR-CM
2026 Alnylam ALN-TTRsc04 Phase 1 data Next-gen TTR silencer (potentially 6-month dosing or improved knockdown)
2026 HELIOS-B 3-year extension data Long-term safety and durability of AMVUTTRA in ATTR-CM
2028 Tafamidis patent expiration Generic entry risk for Pfizer; potential price disruption across class
2026-2027 BridgeBio ATTR-CM international expansion ATTRUBY/BEYONTTRA launches in EU (via Bayer), Japan (via Alexion)
2030 Alnylam 2030 strategy targets $10B+ revenue target driven primarily by TTR franchise scale

headings.text:
1. Disease Overview
Transthyretin (TTR) amyloidosis (ATTR amyloidosis) is a progressive, multisystem disease caused by extracellular deposition of misfolded transthyretin monomers as insoluble amyloid fibrils in tissues including the heart, peripheral nerves, gastrointestinal tract, and kidneys. TTR is a transport protein produced primarily in the liver that carries thyroxine and retinol-binding protein.
Two Forms of ATTR Amyloidosis



Feature
Hereditary ATTR (hATTR / ATTRv)
Wild-Type ATTR (wtATTR)




Cause
>130 known TTR gene mutations (e.g., V30M, V122I, T60A)
Age-related misfolding of normal TTR protein


Age of onset
30-70 years depending on mutation
Typically >65 years, predominantly male


Primary manifestations
Polyneuropathy (hATTR-PN), cardiomyopathy (hATTR-CM), or mixed
Almost exclusively cardiomyopathy (wtATTR-CM)


Prevalence
~50,000 globally; endemic in Portugal, Sweden, Japan, Brazil
Estimated 200,000-300,000 in US alone; dramatically under-diagnosed


Inheritance
Autosomal dominant
Not inherited



Clinical Phenotypes
ATTR Cardiomyopathy (ATTR-CM): The larger market opportunity. Manifests as heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), conduction abnormalities, atrial fibrillation, and carpal tunnel syndrome. The THAOS registry shows ~41% of symptomatic ATTR patients present with predominantly cardiac phenotype. Among older HFpEF patients, 7-13% may have undiagnosed wtATTR-CM. Two-year mortality risk ranges from 10-30% (wtATTR-CM) to 10-50% (variant ATTR-CM). ATTR-CM is the primary commercial battleground, with an estimated US prevalence of ~200,000 patients.
ATTR Polyneuropathy (hATTR-PN / ATTRv-PN): Hereditary only. Progressive sensorimotor and autonomic neuropathy. Staged by FAP (Familial Amyloid Polyneuropathy) staging from Stage 1 (ambulatory) to Stage 3 (wheelchair-bound). Endemic populations exist in Portugal (V30M mutation), Japan (V30M), Sweden (V30M), and Brazil. Smaller market (~50,000 globally) but the original indication for RNAi therapies.
Mixed phenotype: ~17% of patients present with both polyneuropathy and cardiomyopathy. This population is strategically important because it bridges both indications and is where RNAi therapies (which address both) have a differentiation advantage over stabilizers (which are approved only for ATTR-CM).
Diagnosis Landscape
Diagnosis rates are improving but remain low relative to true prevalence. Key diagnostic modalities:

Cardiac imaging: Technetium pyrophosphate (99mTc-PYP) bone scintigraphy for non-invasive diagnosis of ATTR-CM (Grade 2/3 uptake is diagnostic in the absence of a monoclonal protein)
Genetic testing: TTR gene sequencing to distinguish hereditary from wild-type
Endomyocardial biopsy: Gold standard but invasive; used when non-invasive workup is equivocal
Biomarkers: NT-proBNP, troponin T/I for cardiac involvement staging

Alnylam's 2025 strategic imperative #2 focuses on accelerating diagnosis through education on the rapidly progressive nature of disease and multisystem burden.
2. Therapeutic Landscape
Mechanism Classes
The ATTR treatment landscape divides into two fundamental mechanism classes:
TTR Silencers (Gene Silencing): Reduce production of TTR protein at the source (liver). Two sub-types:

RNAi (RNA interference): AMVUTTRA (vutrisiran), ONPATTRO (patisiran) -- Alnylam
ASO (antisense oligonucleotide): Wainua/Wainzua (eplontersen) -- Ionis/AstraZeneca; TEGSEDI (inotersen) -- legacy

TTR Stabilizers: Bind to TTR tetramer to prevent dissociation into amyloidogenic monomers:

Tafamidis: VYNDAQEL/VYNDAMAX (Pfizer) -- dominant incumbent in ATTR-CM
Acoramidis: ATTRUBY (BridgeBio) / BEYONTTRA (ex-US) -- new entrant, claims superior stabilization

Product Profiles
AMVUTTRA (vutrisiran) -- Alnylam

Mechanism: Small interfering RNA (siRNA) targeting TTR mRNA via RNA interference; GalNAc-conjugated for liver-targeted delivery
Administration: Subcutaneous injection, once every 3 months (quarterly)
Approved indications:

hATTR-PN (polyneuropathy): Approved June 2022 (US), September 2022 (EU)
ATTR-CM (cardiomyopathy): Approved March 20, 2025 (US -- first RNAi approved for ATTR-CM), June 2025 (EU)


Key clinical data:

HELIOS-A (hATTR-PN): Non-inferior to ONPATTRO; 48% of patients improved neuropathy from baseline; mean 88% TTR knockdown
HELIOS-B (ATTR-CM): Transformational result. 28% reduction in all-cause mortality + recurrent CV events (overall population); 33% reduction in monotherapy arm. Statistically significant improvements in 6-minute walk test, KCCQ, and NT-proBNP


2025 Revenue: $2,314 million (AMVUTTRA alone); TTR franchise total $2,487 million (including ONPATTRO $173M)
2026 Guidance: TTR franchise $4,400-$4,700 million (83% growth YoY at midpoint)
Patients on therapy: >12,000 as of early 2026
Key differentiators: Only product approved for both ATTR-CM and hATTR-PN; quarterly SC dosing; 88% TTR reduction (highest among silencers); addresses both wild-type and variant TTR

ONPATTRO (patisiran) -- Alnylam

Mechanism: siRNA targeting TTR mRNA; lipid nanoparticle (LNP) formulation
Administration: IV infusion every 3 weeks; requires premedication (corticosteroid, acetaminophen, H1 and H2 blockers)
Approved indication: hATTR-PN (August 2018 US; first RNAi therapeutic ever approved)
Status: Being gradually transitioned to AMVUTTRA. Revenue declining: $173M in 2025 (vs. $2,314M for AMVUTTRA)
Key data: APOLLO trial -- significant improvement vs. placebo in mNIS+7 and Norfolk QoL-DN
Role in franchise: Legacy product, still relevant in France and certain markets where it was first-to-market and has strong neurologist loyalty. Alnylam reps in France equally prioritize AMVUTTRA and ONPATTRO

VYNDAQEL/VYNDAMAX (tafamidis) -- Pfizer

Mechanism: TTR stabilizer; binds to thyroxine-binding sites on TTR tetramer
Administration: Oral, once daily. VYNDAQEL 20mg (original) and VYNDAMAX 61mg (free acid, bioequivalent to 80mg meglumine)
Approved indications: ATTR-CM (May 2019 US); hATTR-PN in some markets
Key clinical data: ATTR-ACT trial: 30% reduction in all-cause mortality over 30 months; 32% reduction in CV-related hospitalizations
2024 Revenue: $5.45 billion (franchise); dominant market leader in ATTR-CM
Market dynamics: VYNDAQEL being discontinued in US (end of 2025); VYNDAMAX continues. Facing competition from ATTRUBY and AMVUTTRA. Patent expiration approaching 2028
Competitive position: Incumbent advantage; oral convenience; 5-year long-term follow-up data. Weaknesses: slower onset of benefit (18+ months to separation from placebo), does not address polyneuropathy, generic threat approaching

ATTRUBY (acoramidis) -- BridgeBio / BEYONTTRA (ex-US)

Mechanism: TTR stabilizer; binds with higher affinity and selectivity than tafamidis
Administration: Oral, once daily (two 200mg capsules)
Approved: November 2024 (US) for ATTR-CM
Key clinical data: ATTRibute-CM trial: significant reduction in all-cause mortality; claims >90% TTR stabilization (vs. ~80% for tafamidis); benefit observed as early as 1 month
2025 Revenue: $362.4 million (full year); rapid uptake
Patients: 7,804 unique prescriptions from 1,856 prescribers as of Feb 2026
Competitive position: Positioned as "best-in-class stabilizer" -- claims faster onset, greater stabilization. Rapidly gaining share in newly diagnosed ATTR-CM. Commercialized internationally by Bayer (Europe) and Alexion (Japan)

Wainua / Wainzua (eplontersen) -- Ionis/AstraZeneca

Mechanism: Ligand-conjugated antisense oligonucleotide (LICA); GalNAc-conjugated for liver targeting
Administration: SC injection, once monthly via auto-injector (self-administered)
Approved: December 2023 (US) for hATTR-PN; EU approval for hATTR-PN (2024)
ATTR-CM: CARDIO-TTRansform Phase 3 study fully enrolled with >1,400 patients (largest ATTR-CM trial ever). Primary completion date extended to April 2026; data expected mid-2026
FDA Fast Track designation: Granted for ATTR-CM
Competitive position: Only self-administered product (auto-injector); monthly dosing vs. quarterly for AMVUTTRA. Direct competitor to AMVUTTRA in polyneuropathy. ATTR-CM data pending -- this is the major upcoming catalyst. Weaker TTR knockdown (~80% at trough vs. ~88% sustained for AMVUTTRA)

TEGSEDI (inotersen) -- Ionis (legacy)

Mechanism: First-generation ASO targeting TTR mRNA
Administration: SC weekly, self-administered; requires platelet monitoring
Status: Being supplanted by Wainua (eplontersen). Still marketed in some European markets
Safety concerns: Risk of thrombocytopenia, glomerulonephritis; requires REMS program in US
Relevance: Declining. Still tracked in ZoomRx France PET data (Q4 2023) but disappearing from later waves

3. Mechanism of Action Deep Dive
Understanding the fundamental mechanism differences is critical for interpreting promotional messaging effectiveness and physician preference data.
Gene Silencing (RNAi and ASO)
RNA interference (RNAi) -- AMVUTTRA and ONPATTRO: Small interfering RNA (siRNA) molecules silence TTR gene expression in hepatocytes. The siRNA binds to complementary TTR messenger RNA (mRNA) and recruits the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC), which cleaves the mRNA and prevents TTR protein synthesis. The result is a dramatic reduction in circulating TTR protein -- both mutant and wild-type. AMVUTTRA uses GalNAc conjugation for efficient liver targeting via the asialoglycoprotein receptor (ASGPR), enabling subcutaneous delivery. ONPATTRO uses lipid nanoparticle (LNP) encapsulation, which requires IV infusion with premedication to prevent infusion-related reactions.
Key pharmacology: AMVUTTRA achieves ~88% mean TTR knockdown within 3 weeks, sustained with quarterly dosing. This rapid and deep suppression is the basis of Alnylam's "rapid knockdown" messaging narrative -- one of the most motivating messages in PET data across all markets, yet consistently under-delivered by reps.
Antisense oligonucleotides (ASO) -- Wainua and TEGSEDI: Single-stranded modified DNA or RNA molecules that bind complementary TTR mRNA via Watson-Crick base pairing and recruit RNase H1 to degrade the mRNA. Wainua (eplontersen) uses ligand-conjugated antisense (LICA) technology with GalNAc conjugation, similar to AMVUTTRA's liver targeting approach but through a different gene silencing pathway. Achieves ~80% TTR reduction at trough. TEGSEDI (inotersen) was the first-generation ASO without conjugation, requiring higher doses and carrying more safety risks (thrombocytopenia, glomerulonephritis).
TTR Stabilization (Tafamidis and Acoramidis)
Stabilizers bind to the thyroxine-binding sites on the TTR tetramer, preventing its dissociation into pro-amyloidogenic monomers. Unlike silencers, stabilizers do not reduce TTR production -- they keep existing TTR in its native, non-toxic tetrameric form. This means amyloid deposits already present continue to exist but further deposition is slowed.
Tafamidis (VYNDAQEL/VYNDAMAX): First-generation stabilizer. Achieves ~80% TTR stabilization. The ATTR-ACT trial showed a 30% mortality reduction over 30 months, but the Kaplan-Meier curves did not separate until ~18 months -- a slower onset than physicians prefer. Available as oral once-daily formulation, which is the primary convenience advantage.
Acoramidis (ATTRUBY/BEYONTTRA): Second-generation stabilizer. Claims >90% TTR stabilization (higher selectivity and binding affinity than tafamidis). The ATTRibute-CM trial showed clinical benefit as early as 1 month, addressing the tafamidis "slow onset" weakness. This "fastest onset" positioning is BridgeBio's primary competitive weapon against both tafamidis and AMVUTTRA.
Silencer vs. Stabilizer: The Strategic Choice
The fundamental clinical question is whether reducing TTR production (silencing) or stabilizing existing TTR (stabilization) produces better long-term outcomes. Arguments for each:
Silencing advantages:

Addresses the root cause by reducing TTR production at the source
Effective against both mutant and wild-type TTR equally
Covers both cardiac and neurological manifestations
88% reduction in circulating TTR (vs. preventing ~80-90% from misfolding)
HELIOS-B showed mortality benefit in ATTR-CM, validating the cardiac efficacy of the gene silencing approach

Stabilizer advantages:

Oral administration (daily pill vs. injection)
No vitamin A supplementation required (silencers reduce retinol-binding protein alongside TTR)
Longer real-world track record (tafamidis approved 2019 for ATTR-CM)
Physician familiarity, especially among cardiologists accustomed to oral cardiovascular medications
Potentially lower cost (generic tafamidis expected post-2028)

The emerging consensus in treatment guidelines (ACC 2025 Concise Clinical Guidance) is that both classes have a role, with the treatment choice depending on disease phenotype (CM vs. PN vs. mixed), severity, patient preference for administration route, and increasingly, the potential for combination therapy.
4. Competitive Dynamics
The Three-Way Battle in ATTR-CM (2025-2026)
The ATTR-CM market is now a three-way competition, with Wainua potentially entering as a fourth:


VYNDAMAX (tafamidis) -- the incumbent: $5.45B franchise (2024). Oral convenience. But losing share to newer entrants. Patent cliff approaching 2028. Pfizer discontinuing VYNDAQEL (low-dose) in US by end 2025, consolidating to VYNDAMAX only


AMVUTTRA (vutrisiran) -- the disruptor: HELIOS-B data positions it as first disease-modifying therapy with mortality benefit via gene silencing. Quarterly SC dosing. Only product bridging CM and PN. Explosive revenue growth ($2.3B in 2025, guided to $4.4-4.7B in 2026)


ATTRUBY (acoramidis) -- the challenger: "Better stabilizer" narrative. Oral daily. $362M in first full year. Gaining traction with newly diagnosed patients. Claims faster onset (1 month) and >90% stabilization


Wainua (eplontersen) -- the pending entrant: CARDIO-TTRansform data expected mid-2026. Monthly SC self-administered. If positive, creates direct head-to-head silencer competition with AMVUTTRA


Key Strategic Questions

Silencer vs. Stabilizer: Will the market stratify by mechanism, with silencers preferred for mixed phenotype/PN and stabilizers preferred for pure CM? Or will HELIOS-B's mortality data position AMVUTTRA as universal first-line?
Combination therapy: Will silencer + stabilizer combinations become standard of care? ZoomRx PCA data already tracks AMVUTTRA + VYNDAMAX and AMVUTTRA + ATTRUBY combinations
Wainua's ATTR-CM entry: If CARDIO-TTRansform is positive, Wainua becomes the only self-administered silencer with ATTR-CM approval. Self-administration is a meaningful differentiator for patient convenience
Access and affordability: Insurance coverage, formulary status, and out-of-pocket costs are significant competitive battlegrounds. Alnylam reports 99% US coverage and 70% of patients paying $0 out-of-pocket

The diagnosed population is growing 15-20% annually due to improved awareness, non-invasive cardiac imaging (Tc-PYP scintigraphy), and increased genetic testing. Alnylam estimates ~200,000 ATTR-CM patients in the US; at current diagnosis rates, the addressable treated population is a fraction of this. This diagnosis gap is why Alnylam's Strategic Imperative #2 focuses on "driving urgency to diagnose."
International Market Dynamics
Based on ZoomRx international PET data, the key ex-US markets each have distinct characteristics:
France: Strong neurology heritage. ONPATTRO was established early, creating deep Alnylam loyalty among neurologists. Alnylam's total SOV leads Pfizer (55% vs. 45% among neurologists in Q2 2024). Average hATTR patient load per physician is relatively high (~6-8 patients). Practice setting skewed community (37%) vs. academic (63%) in W1, with neuro-only recruitment in W2.
Italy: Balanced between cardiology and neurology. Higher academic setting (70%). AMVUTTRA's reach declined between waves (58% to 51%), while VYNDAQEL maintained reach (62%). Average patient load increased from 9 to 14 between waves, suggesting growing diagnosis rates. NPS for both products declined between waves, suggesting increased competitive noise.
Germany: Community-practice dominant (69% in Q4 2024). Smaller average patient loads (~3-4). Alnylam and Pfizer at near-parity in reach (71% each) but Pfizer maintains marginal SOV advantage (53% vs. 47%). Germany is price-sensitive -- Alnylam flagged ~$25M quarterly revenue reduction from German price cuts in 2026.
Spain: Alnylam's strongest international market. Predominantly academic setting (96%). AMVUTTRA achieved the most dramatic competitive turnaround -- SOV flipped from 35% to 56% between Q4 2023 and Q4 2024. Average patient load increased from 6 to 13, suggesting rapidly growing diagnosis. Alnylam reach hit 91%, the highest of any market.
Japan: Pfizer-dominant. Tafamidis has historical presence (approved for hATTR-PN before many markets). Very low average patient loads (~2 per physician). VYNDAQEL/VYNMAC maintains 59% SOV and 84% reach vs. AMVUTTRA's 41% SOV and 72% reach. However, AMVUTTRA reps drive stronger perception improvement when they do reach physicians (78% vs. 57%), indicating an access problem rather than a message problem.
Patient Journey in ATTR-CM
The typical ATTR-CM patient journey, as informed by ZoomRx PCA data and the ADOPT funnel framework:


Suspicion and Referral: Patient presents with HFpEF, unexplained LVH, aortic stenosis, carpal tunnel syndrome, or spinal stenosis. Increasingly, advanced imaging and biomarkers trigger ATTR workup. Mean time from symptom onset to diagnosis: 2-4 years (improving but still delayed)


Diagnosis: Tc-PYP scintigraphy (non-invasive, high sensitivity/specificity). If Grade 2/3 uptake + no monoclonal protein on serum/urine testing, ATTR-CM can be diagnosed without biopsy. Genetic testing to distinguish wtATTR from hATTR


Treatment Decision: Historically tafamidis was the only disease-modifying option. Now three choices: VYNDAMAX (oral stabilizer), ATTRUBY (oral stabilizer, "better"), AMVUTTRA (SC injection, silencer). Treatment selection increasingly influenced by phenotype (pure CM vs. mixed), severity, age, and comorbidities


Ongoing Management: Heart failure optimization (diuretics, MRAs, SGLT2i), arrhythmia management, multidisciplinary care (cardiology, neurology, genetic counseling). Monitoring TTR levels (for silencer-treated patients), NT-proBNP, 6-minute walk test, KCCQ


Therapy Optimization: Emerging question of whether to add a second agent (silencer + stabilizer) or switch MOA class if progression occurs. ZoomRx PCA already capturing combination use patterns


6. Alnylam Strategic Imperatives (2025-2026)
Based on the Q1 2025 Integration of Market Research deck, Alnylam's US TTR Business Unit operates on four strategic imperatives:

Establish AMVUTTRA as first-line treatment of choice -- Differentiate as new SOC in 1L ATTR-CM, foundational therapy across treatment paradigm, RNAi as MOA of choice, rapid TTR knockdown narrative, real-world experience
Drive urgency to diagnose, treat, and optimize therapy -- Accelerate diagnosis via education on disease progression, support understanding of progression on therapy, optimize clinical workflows
Optimize customer experience and minimize non-clinical barriers -- Formulary approval, reduce administrative/site barriers, build confidence in access/affordability
Establish leadership in cardiology/ATTR -- Expand presence in cardiology and heart failure, build champions, build interest in TTR pipeline (ALN-TTRsc04 and beyond)

Strategic Imperative to Research Mapping
The Q1 2025 Integration deck maps each imperative to specific ZoomRx research deliverables:
Imperative 1 (AMVUTTRA as 1L): ATU tracks AMVUTTRA awareness and perceptions for ATTR-CM, patient share, and drivers/barriers. PET tracks branded and unbranded message recall, likelihood to prescribe post rep interaction, and product perceptions of MOA (stabilizer vs. silencer). PCA captures treatment journey, consideration set, source of share, and key drivers to prescribing.
Imperative 2 (Diagnosis urgency): ATU tracks HCP alignment to disease beliefs, tests and assessments used. PET tracks recall of disease state messages and impact of disease education on familiarity. PCA tracks treatment journey and usage of tests across patient profiles.
Imperative 3 (Minimize barriers): ATU tracks perceptions of administration burden, access/affordability. PET tracks recall of access messages and blinded message association for access/cost content.
Imperative 4 (Cardiology leadership): ATU tracks brand equity. PET tracks manufacturer perceptions and impact on prescribing behavior. This is the imperative most directly tied to the cardiologist reach gap identified in international PET data.
7. ZoomRx Research Program Architecture
ZoomRx runs three complementary tracking studies for the Alnylam ATTR franchise:
US Studies

PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracker): Quarterly personal promotions + semi-annual NPP. Tracks AMVUTTRA-CM, AMVUTTRA-PN, VYNDAMAX, ATTRUBY, and WAINUA. 90 interactions per product per quarter (CM), 60 per semi-annual wave (PN). Measures reach, frequency, SOV, message recall, message effectiveness, rep performance, product perception change, likelihood to Rx
ATU (Awareness, Trial, Usage): Quarterly for ATTR-CM (90 cardiologists), one wave for hATTR-PN (80 physicians, 50/50 cardio/neuro). Tracks awareness, familiarity, drivers/barriers, product performance, stated vs. derived importance, NPS
PCA (Patient Chart Audit): Semi-annual. 300 charts CM+Mixed, 100 charts PN. Treatment journey, consideration set, source of share, new-to-brand Rx, combination therapy patterns

International PET Studies
Multi-country promotional effectiveness tracking across France, Italy, Germany, Spain, and Japan. Two waves completed:

Wave 1 (Q4 2023): France, Italy, Japan, Germany, Spain -- baseline measurements
Wave 2 (Q2 2024 for France/Italy; Q4 2024 for Spain/Germany/Japan) -- trend tracking

Products tracked: AMVUTTRA, ONPATTRO (France only), VYNDAQEL/VYNDAMAX, TEGSEDI (France W1 only)
Integrated Analysis
Quarterly integration of PET+ATU+PCA data into the HCP Adoption (ADOPT) funnel framework: Awareness --> Depth of Knowledge --> Overall Product Perception --> Product Usage --> Treatment Advocacy. Each study feeds different stages of the funnel.
8. Key Metrics and Definitions

SOV (Share of Voice): Ratio of brand details to total market details. Primary competitive metric
Message Effectiveness (ME): Geometric mean of Differentiation, Believability, and Motivation scores (6,7 on 7-point scale)
Reach: % of physicians reporting at least one rep interaction in last 60 days
Frequency: Average number of interactions per physician in last 60 days
NPS (Net Promoter Score): % promoters (9-10) minus % detractors (0-6) on likelihood to recommend
TTR Knockdown: % reduction in serum TTR protein levels -- key efficacy biomarker for silencers. AMVUTTRA achieves ~88% mean knockdown
TTR Stabilization: % of TTR tetramer stabilized -- key efficacy biomarker for stabilizers. ATTRUBY claims >90%, tafamidis ~80%

9. Pipeline and Future Catalysts
